title: Philip II of Spain

infobox.value: 21 May 1527 [Palacio de Pimentel](/wiki/Palacio_de_Pimentel "Palacio de Pimentel"), [Valladolid](/wiki/Valladolid "Valladolid"), [Crown of Castile](/wiki/Crown_of_Castile "Crown of Castile")
13 September 1598(1598-09-13) (aged 71) [El Escorial](/wiki/El_Escorial "El Escorial"), [San Lorenzo de El Escorial](/wiki/San_Lorenzo_de_El_Escorial "San Lorenzo de El Escorial"), Crown of Castile

[Maria Manuela of Portugal](/wiki/Maria_Manuela_of_Portugal "Maria Manuela of Portugal")  ​  ​ (m. 1543; died 1545)​      [Mary I of England](/wiki/Mary_I_of_England "Mary I of England")  ​  ​ (m. 1554; died 1558)​      [Elisabeth of Valois](/wiki/Elisabeth_of_Valois "Elisabeth of Valois")  ​  ​ (m. 1559; died 1568)​      [Anna of Austria](/wiki/Anna_of_Austria,_Queen_of_Spain "Anna of Austria, Queen of Spain")  ​  ​ (m. 1570; died 1580)​
[Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor")

([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Felipe el Prudente*), was [King of Spain](/wiki/King_of_Spain "King of Spain")[[note 2]](#cite_note-2) from 1556, [King of Portugal](/wiki/List_of_Portuguese_monarchs "List of Portuguese monarchs") from 1580, and [King of Naples](/wiki/List_of_Neapolitan_monarchs "List of Neapolitan monarchs") and [Sicily](/wiki/List_of_Sicilian_monarchs "List of Sicilian monarchs") from 1554 until his death in 1598. He was also *[jure uxoris](/wiki/Jure_uxoris "Jure uxoris")* [King of England](/wiki/List_of_English_monarchs "List of English monarchs") and [Ireland](/wiki/List_of_Irish_monarchs "List of Irish monarchs") from [his marriage](/wiki/Wedding_of_Mary_I_of_England_and_Philip_of_Spain "Wedding of Mary I of England and Philip of Spain") to Queen [Mary I](/wiki/Mary_I "Mary I") in 1554 until her death in 1558. Further, he was [Duke of Milan](/wiki/Duke_of_Milan "Duke of Milan") from 1540. From 1555, he was Lord of the [Seventeen Provinces](/wiki/Seventeen_Provinces "Seventeen Provinces") of the [Netherlands](/wiki/Habsburg_Netherlands "Habsburg Netherlands").

**Philip II**[[note 1]](#cite_note-1) (21 May 1527 – 13 September 1598), sometimes known in Spain as **Philip the Prudent** ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Felipe el Prudente*), was [King of Spain](/wiki/King_of_Spain "King of Spain")[[note 2]](#cite_note-2) from 1556, [King of Portugal](/wiki/List_of_Portuguese_monarchs "List of Portuguese monarchs") from 1580, and [King of Naples](/wiki/List_of_Neapolitan_monarchs "List of Neapolitan monarchs") and [Sicily](/wiki/List_of_Sicilian_monarchs "List of Sicilian monarchs") from 1554 until his death in 1598. He was also *[jure uxoris](/wiki/Jure_uxoris "Jure uxoris")* [King of England](/wiki/List_of_English_monarchs "List of English monarchs") and [Ireland](/wiki/List_of_Irish_monarchs "List of Irish monarchs") from [his marriage](/wiki/Wedding_of_Mary_I_of_England_and_Philip_of_Spain "Wedding of Mary I of England and Philip of Spain") to Queen [Mary I](/wiki/Mary_I "Mary I") in 1554 until her death in 1558. Further, he was [Duke of Milan](/wiki/Duke_of_Milan "Duke of Milan") from 1540. From 1555, he was Lord of the [Seventeen Provinces](/wiki/Seventeen_Provinces "Seventeen Provinces") of the [Netherlands](/wiki/Habsburg_Netherlands "Habsburg Netherlands").

The son of [Emperor Charles V](/wiki/Emperor_Charles_V "Emperor Charles V") and [Isabella of Portugal](/wiki/Isabella_of_Portugal,_Holy_Roman_Empress "Isabella of Portugal, Holy Roman Empress"), Philip inherited his father's [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") in 1556, and succeeded to the [Portuguese throne](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Portugal "Kingdom of Portugal") in 1580 following a dynastic crisis, forming the [Iberian Union](/wiki/Iberian_Union "Iberian Union"). The Spanish conquests [of the Inca Empire](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_the_Inca_Empire "Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire") and of the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), named in his honour by [Ruy López de Villalobos](/wiki/Ruy_L%C3%B3pez_de_Villalobos "Ruy López de Villalobos"), were completed during his reign. He finished building the royal palace [El Escorial](/wiki/El_Escorial "El Escorial") in 1584. Under Philip II, Spain reached the height of its influence and power, advancing into the [Spanish Golden Age](/wiki/Spanish_Golden_Age "Spanish Golden Age"), and ruled territories in every continent then known to Europeans. Deeply devout, Philip saw himself as the defender of Catholic Europe against the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") and the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"), and invested Spain's position as the leading [European power](/wiki/Great_power "Great power") in multiple simultaneous warring efforts.

During his reign, Spain participated in notable victories against the Ottomans in [Oran](/wiki/Sieges_of_Oran_and_Mers_El_K%C3%A9bir "Sieges of Oran and Mers El Kébir"), [Malta](/wiki/Great_Siege_of_Malta "Great Siege of Malta") and [Lepanto](/wiki/Battle_of_Lepanto "Battle of Lepanto"). In 1584, during the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War"), Philip signed the [Treaty of Joinville](/wiki/Treaty_of_Joinville "Treaty of Joinville"), funding the French [Catholic League](/wiki/Catholic_League_(French) "Catholic League (French)") over the following decade against the French [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenots "Huguenots"). In 1588, he sent an [armada](/wiki/Spanish_Armada "Spanish Armada") to invade Protestant England, with the strategic aim of overthrowing his former sister-in-law [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I") and re-establishing Catholicism there, but his fleet was repulsed in a skirmish and wrecked by storms as it returned to Spain. Philip's naval power recovered after the defeat of a similarly sized [English Armada](/wiki/English_Armada "English Armada") sent against Spain. As a consequence of these conflicts, Philip led a highly [debt-leveraged regime](/wiki/National_debt "National debt"), seeing [state defaults](/wiki/Sovereign_default "Sovereign default") in 1557, 1560, 1569, 1575, and 1596. An ambitious plan to extend his conquests to [China and across Asia](/wiki/Empresa_de_China "Empresa de China") was also considered and abandoned.

Historical reception of Philip II became heavily influenced by enemy [propaganda](/wiki/Propaganda "Propaganda"), which he refused to answer or defend himself against, even prohibiting biographical accounts of his life. His negative foreign reputation eventually developed into the [Spanish Black Legend](/wiki/Black_legend "Black legend"), while reappraisals of his figure would later entangle in turn with the [White Legend](/wiki/White_legend "White legend"). As a result, historian [Helmut Koenigsberger](/wiki/Helmut_Koenigsberger "Helmut Koenigsberger") would write about Philip, "there has, perhaps, been no personality in modern history, not even [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") or [Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin"), who has been both as enigmatic and controversial as Philip II of Spain... Neither his own contemporaries nor later historians have been able to agree on his character, his aims or even the degree of success he achieved."[[1]](#cite_note-3)

## Early life: 1527–1548

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Philip_II_of_Spain&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life: 1527–1548")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Bautizo_Felipe_II_grande.jpg/250px-Bautizo_Felipe_II_grande.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bautizo_Felipe_II_grande.jpg)

*The Baptism of Philip II* in [Valladolid](/wiki/Valladolid "Valladolid"), Castile. Historical ceiling preserved in [Palacio de Pimentel](/wiki/Palacio_de_Pimentel "Palacio de Pimentel") (Valladolid).

A member of the [House of Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg"), Philip was the son of [Emperor Charles V](/wiki/Emperor_Charles_V "Emperor Charles V"), who was also king of [Castile](/wiki/Crown_of_Castile "Crown of Castile") and [Aragon](/wiki/Crown_of_Aragon "Crown of Aragon"), and [Isabella of Portugal](/wiki/Isabella_of_Portugal,_Holy_Roman_Empress "Isabella of Portugal, Holy Roman Empress"). He was born in the Castilian capital of [Valladolid](/wiki/Valladolid "Valladolid") on 21 May 1527 at [Palacio de Pimentel](/wiki/Palacio_de_Pimentel "Palacio de Pimentel"),[[2]](#cite_note-4) which was owned by Don Bernardino Pimentel (the first Marqués de Távara). The culture and courtly life of Castile were an important influence in his early life. He was entrusted to the royal governess [Leonor de Mascareñas](/wiki/Leonor_de_Mascare%C3%B1as "Leonor de Mascareñas"), and tutored by [Juan Martínez Siliceo](/wiki/Juan_Mart%C3%ADnez_Siliceo "Juan Martínez Siliceo"), the future [archbishop of Toledo](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Toledo "Archbishop of Toledo"). Philip displayed reasonable aptitude in arts and letters alike. Later he would study with more illustrious tutors, including [humanists](/wiki/Renaissance_humanism "Renaissance humanism") like [Juan Martínez Silíceo](/wiki/Juan_Mart%C3%ADnez_Sil%C3%ADceo "Juan Martínez Silíceo") and Juan Cristóbal Calvete de Estrella.[[3]](#cite_note-Humanism-5) Though Philip had good command over [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), Spanish, and Portuguese, he never managed to equal his father as a [polyglot](/wiki/Polyglot "Polyglot"). While Philip was also an [archduke of Austria](/wiki/Archduke_of_Austria "Archduke of Austria"), he was seen as a foreigner in the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"). The feeling was mutual. Philip felt himself to be culturally Spanish; he had been born in Castile and raised in the Castilian court, his native language was Spanish, and he preferred to live in the Spanish kingdoms. This ultimately impeded his succession to the imperial throne.[[4]](#cite_note-JBE-6)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

In April 1528, when Philip was eleven months old, he received the oath of allegiance as heir to the crown from the [Cortes](/wiki/Cortes_Generales "Cortes Generales") of Castile. From that time until the death of his mother Isabella in 1539, he was raised in the royal court of Castile under the care of his mother and one of her [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_people "Portuguese people") ladies, Doña Leonor de Mascarenhas, to whom he was devotedly attached. Philip was also close to his two sisters, [María](/wiki/Maria_of_Austria,_Holy_Roman_Empress "Maria of Austria, Holy Roman Empress") and [Juana](/wiki/Joan_of_Austria,_Princess_of_Portugal "Joan of Austria, Princess of Portugal"), and to his two pages, the Portuguese nobleman [Rui Gomes da Silva](/wiki/Ruy_G%C3%B3mez_de_Silva "Ruy Gómez de Silva") and [Luis de Requesens y Zúñiga](/wiki/Luis_de_Requesens_y_Z%C3%BA%C3%B1iga "Luis de Requesens y Zúñiga"), the son of his governor. These men would serve Philip throughout their lives, as would [Antonio Pérez](/wiki/Antonio_P%C3%A9rez_(statesman) "Antonio Pérez (statesman)"), his [secretary](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_(Ancient_Regime_in_Spain) "Secretary of State (Ancient Regime in Spain)") from 1541.

Philip's martial training was undertaken by his governor, [Juan de Zúñiga y Requesens](/wiki/Juan_de_Z%C3%BA%C3%B1iga_y_Requesens "Juan de Zúñiga y Requesens"), a Castilian nobleman who served as the *[commendador](/wiki/Commendador "Commendador") mayor* of Castile. The practical lessons in warfare were overseen by [Fernando Álvarez de Toledo, 3rd Duke of Alba](/wiki/Fernando_%C3%81lvarez_de_Toledo,_3rd_Duke_of_Alba "Fernando Álvarez de Toledo, 3rd Duke of Alba") during the [Italian Wars](/wiki/Italian_War_of_1542%E2%80%931546 "Italian War of 1542–1546"). Philip was present at the [Siege of Perpignan](/wiki/Siege_of_Perpignan_(1542) "Siege of Perpignan (1542)") in 1542 but did not see action as the Spanish army under Alba decisively defeated the besieging French forces under the [Dauphin of France](/wiki/Henry_II_of_France "Henry II of France"). On his way back to Castile, Philip received the oath of allegiance of the [Aragonese Cortes](/wiki/Cortes_of_Aragon#Early_Cortes "Cortes of Aragon") at [Monzón](/wiki/Monz%C3%B3n "Monzón").

His political training had begun a year previously under his father, who had found his son studious, grave, and prudent beyond his years, and having decided to train and initiate him in the government of the Spanish kingdoms. The king-emperor's interactions with his son during his stay in Castile convinced him of Philip's precocity in statesmanship, so he determined to leave in his hands the [regency of the Spanish kingdoms](/wiki/List_of_Spanish_regents "List of Spanish regents") in 1543. Philip, who had been made the [Duke of Milan](/wiki/Duke_of_Milan "Duke of Milan") in 1540, began governing the most extensive empire in the world at the young age of sixteen.

Charles left Philip with experienced advisors—notably the secretary [Francisco de los Cobos](/wiki/Francisco_de_los_Cobos "Francisco de los Cobos") and the general, the Duke of Alba. Philip was also left with extensive written instructions that emphasised "piety, patience, modesty, and distrust". These principles of Charles were gradually assimilated by his son, who would grow up to become grave, self-possessed and cautious. Personally, Philip spoke softly and had an icy self-mastery; in the words of one of his ministers, "he had a smile that was cut by a sword".[[5]](#cite_note-7) [*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

### Philip's journey to Brussels (1548–1549)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Philip_II_of_Spain&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Philip's journey to Brussels (1548–1549)")]

On 1 October 1548, Philip departed Valladolid on his voyage, appointing his sister [Maria](/wiki/Maria_of_Austria,_Holy_Roman_Empress "Maria of Austria, Holy Roman Empress") and her husband [Maximilian](/wiki/Maximilian_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Maximilian II, Holy Roman Emperor") as regents in his absence. He began a formal journey through [Aragon](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Aragon "Kingdom of Aragon") and [Catalonia](/wiki/Principality_of_Catalonia "Principality of Catalonia"), visiting religious sites such as [Zaragoza](/wiki/Zaragoza "Zaragoza") and Montserrat, and receiving homage in [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona") and [Girona](/wiki/Girona "Girona").[[6]](#cite_note-HumeRosas-8)

In November 1548, Philip travelled with [Diego López de Medrano](/wiki/Diego_L%C3%B3pez_de_Medrano_y_Vergara#Diego_López_de_Medrano "Diego López de Medrano y Vergara"), his first or chief [equerry](/wiki/Caballerizo_mayor "Caballerizo mayor"), to [Rosas](/wiki/Roses,_Spain "Roses, Spain") and [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa") aboard the [galley](/wiki/Galley "Galley") belonging to Antonio de Toledo, the senior equerry.[[7]](#cite_note-9)[[8]](#cite_note-10) Rosas was his point of departure for Italy.[[9]](#cite_note-11) At the Bay of Rosas, he was met by [Andrea Doria](/wiki/Andrea_Doria "Andrea Doria") with a fleet of fifty-eight galleys.[[6]](#cite_note-HumeRosas-8) Florentine obsequies depict his journey in large canvases depicting formative episodes from Philip's youth, including Philip's departure for Brussels from Rosas on the galley, and Philip's reception by the Doge of Genoa in 1548.[[10]](#cite_note-12) The admiral, moved by the occasion, knelt before Philip and quoted [Simeon](/wiki/Simeon_(Gospel_of_Luke) "Simeon (Gospel of Luke)"): "Now, Lord, let Thy servant depart in peace, for his eyes have seen Thy salvation."[[6]](#cite_note-HumeRosas-8)

Philip landed at [Savona](/wiki/Savona "Savona") and proceeded to Genoa, where he stayed at the Doria palace and received papal emissary [Octavio Farnese](/wiki/Ottavio_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma "Ottavio Farnese, Duke of Parma"), who presented him with a ceremonial sword and cap of state from [Pope Paul III](/wiki/Pope_Paul_III "Pope Paul III").[[6]](#cite_note-HumeRosas-8) The gesture, despite political tensions, reflected hopes that Philip would serve as a future protector of the Church. Celebrated in [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), [Mantua](/wiki/Mantua "Mantua"), and other cities, he continued through the [Tyrol](/wiki/Tyrol "Tyrol") and [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") before reaching Brussels to meet Emperor Charles V on 1 April 1549.[[6]](#cite_note-HumeRosas-8)

## Domestic policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Philip_II_of_Spain&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Domestic policy")]

|  |  |
